data.title: 8 arrested over attempting to defraud HK$8.24 million from Hong Kong subsidy scheme
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested eight people for allegedly attempting to defraud HK$8.24 million (US$1.06 million) from a subsidy scheme meant to help companies boost employees’ technology skills, with a family suspected of running the swindle.
Chen Chi-cheong, assistant district commander (crime) of Sham Shui Po police district, said on Friday that officers had busted a suspected fraud syndicate this week.
The eight suspects were believed to have targeted the government’s “New Industrialisation and Technology Training Programme” between 2022 and 2024.
“The syndicate established companies that had no substantial operations, showing an organised and systematic attempt to defraud public funds intended for promoting innovation,” Chen said.
The suspects consisted of six men and two women, aged 33 to 78. They include five members of the same family, comprising a couple, their two sons and a partner of one son.
The force said the family were suspected of being the scheme’s masterminds.
Chief Inspector Cheng Ki-fung said the Vocational Training Council (VTC), which was the programme’s secretariat, had referred the case to the police at the end of 2024 after an ad hoc check of some participants had raised suspicions.
Under the scheme’s rules, training providers and companies could apply for subsidies if they enrolled full-time staff to take part in technology-related courses. But participating companies could not be associated with programme organisers.
During the check, three participants told the VTC that they were hired as part-time secret reviewers to evaluate the programme and had not realised they were supposed to be representing an employer when attending the training as full-time staff.
Cheng said the investigation suggested the family had set up a company to provide training courses for “employees” of 11 other businesses they had founded.
They then applied for subsidies through both the course provider and the other companies, which recruited “staff members” via social media to attend the courses, he said.
The suspects also collected the personal details of the people they hired and told the secretariat that relevant employees were all full-time staff at their network of companies, registering them for courses operated by the syndicate’s programme provider.
The chief inspector said officers had discovered that the companies involved in this case had submitted false documents to the secretariat, including fake employment and training attendance records.
“Our investigations have discovered there were 12 companies suspected of conspiracy to defraud the relevant training subsidies,” Cheng said.
“The relationships between these companies have seriously violated the conflict of interest declaration under the scheme.”
Police said the 12 businesses allegedly linked to the suspects had applied for subsidies worth HK$8.24 million between August 2022 and December 2024.
The companies only received HK$640,000 of the funds, with the force preventing the rest of the money from being disbursed as part of its investigations.
Senior Inspector Wan Tin-sing said officers had confiscated 11 computers suspected to contain relevant documents, as well as company stamps, chequebooks and other documents while making arrests this week.
Wan said the eight arrestees claimed to be businesspeople, homemakers or unemployed.
In Hong Kong, the maximum penalty for conspiracy to defraud is 14 years in prison.
Alexander Leung Siu-kwun, senior manager of the Innovation and Technology Commission’s innovation and technology fund, said the scheme was rolled out in August 2018 to subsidise local enterprises that enrolled their staff in high-end technology training related to emerging industries.
He said each participating company could have received up to HK$250,000 worth of subsidies per financial year, with each employee permitted to be subsidised for one course for the same period.
“The secretariat has set up a stringent evaluation mechanism, including ad hoc spot checks,” Leung said.

data.title: Are the Communist Party and the KMT reviving a cross-strait political channel?
data.description:
The Communist Party and Taiwan’s Kuomintang (KMT) appear to be in the final stages of talks to revive an inter-party forum suspended nine years ago.
The three-day forum, designed to facilitate party-to-party communication and promote cross-strait exchanges, was scheduled to start in Beijing on January 27, Taiwan’s China Times newspaper reported on Friday.
If it takes place, it will be the first time the event has been held since 2016, when it was suspended because of tensions between the mainland and Taiwan and debate within the KMT. It was held 11 times before then.
The development represents a renewed effort by Beijing and Taiwan’s main opposition party to restore inter-party channels at a critical moment.
Cross-strait relations began declining in 2016 when Tsai Ing-wen became the island’s leader and have deteriorated under her successor, William Lai Ching-te.
Late last year, the People’s Liberation Army staged a round of massive military drills near Taiwan, saying they were aimed at deterring “Taiwan independence forces” and external intervention.
The forum is also expected to pave the way for a meeting between newly elected KMT chairwoman Cheng Li-wun and Chinese President Xi Jinping, which some media outlets have suggested could occur as soon as March.
Cheng has repeatedly expressed hopes for a face-to-face meeting with Xi and supported the idea of a strong Chinese identity and reconciliation with the mainland. She said on Friday that she would give more details about the forum on Saturday.
Cheng’s deputy, KMT vice-chairman Hsiao Hsu-tsen, is expected to head the Taiwanese delegation at the forum, according to Taiwan media reports.
Beijing’s Taiwan Affairs Office did not respond to a request for comment.


The forum was first held in 2006 and called the Cross-Strait Economic and Trade Forum.
It followed the historic 2005 meeting in Beijing between then-Communist Party general secretary Hu Jintao and then-KMT chairman Lien Chan, which resulted in a five-point consensus, including the establishment of a party-to-party communication platform.
Over the following decade, proposals from the event covered a wide range of issues, including agriculture, industrial and financial cooperation, tourism and people-to-people exchanges.
The forum was renamed the Cross-Strait Peaceful Development Forum in November 2016, just months after the Democratic Progressive Party took power in Taiwan. Then-KMT leader Hung Hsiu-chu took part in the gathering and met Xi before the forum as part of her trip to the mainland.
Since then, there has been a lack of communication channels between the KMT and the Communist Party. There have also been no official channels between the two sides of the strait since the DPP came to power.
Zhang Wensheng, deputy dean and a professor at the Taiwan Research Institute at Xiamen University, said the forum would help to ease cross-strait tensions as well as promote cross-strait exchanges.
But given the KMT was not in power, “it would have little ability to resolve the fundamental political differences across the strait”, Zhang said.
According to mainland cross-strait analyst Zhu Songling, the forum would be Cheng’s first effort to navigate cross-strait relations and consolidate her authority within the party.
But Zhu warned that there would be few breakthroughs.
“Given the timing and cross-strait political atmosphere, if the forum does go ahead, the political symbolism will far outweigh substantive administrative breakthroughs,” said Zhu, a professor at the Institute of Taiwan Studies at Beijing Union University.


Beijing has repeatedly criticised Lai’s pro-independence stance and launched four large-scale military exercises since he took office in May 2024. The latest, a two-day drill dubbed “Justice Mission-2025”, was held at the end of December.
On Friday, the DPP raised doubts about the event, asking what the KMT might be offering in exchange for attending the forum.
Citing the KMT’s decision to block Taiwan’s defence budget in the Legislative Yuan, the DPP said: “Taiwan’s security should not be used as leverage to curry favour with the Chinese Communist Party.”
Zhu also noted that, because the KMT was not the ruling party, any outcomes from the forum – such as resuming tourism or flights – could become “empty promises” if the DPP refused to implement them.
KMT spokesman Niu Hsu-ting said on Friday that if the forum could be reconvened, it would mean there was still a channel for dialogue between the two parties.
“The outside world should view it with a more open mind, especially given the rapidly changing international situation and rising geopolitical risks. Communication itself is necessary,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong sets up 13-member expert panel to help mainland firms expand overseas
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Department of Justice has set up a 13-member expert committee to advise on strategies for promoting the city’s legal and professional services sector to mainland Chinese businesses seeking to expand overseas.
The department on Friday said the new Expert Committee on Professional Services for Going Global, chaired by Deputy Secretary for Justice Horace Cheung Kwok-kwan, would build upon the GoGlobal platform authorities launched in December.
Cheung said the committee would gather “strategic advice from members of different professional services sectors” to help mainland enterprises.
The justice department said the 13 members would serve a two-year term and comprise experts from the legal, financial and accounting sectors from the mainland and Hong Kong, as well as business and corporate representatives.
Among the members for the first two-year term were Legislative Councillor Maggie Chan Man-ki and Hong Kong Trade Development Council deputy executive director Patrick Lau Hui-ping.
The department said the committee would provide advice and help on enhancing the understanding of the needs of mainland enterprises expanding overseas.

It would also coordinate with Hong Kong’s professional services sectors, including the legal, accounting and financial sectors, to support mainland enterprises going global.
The establishment of the expert committee follows the launch of the Hong Kong Professional Services GoGlobal Platform last month.
The platform aims to coordinate the city’s professional services to meet the needs of mainland enterprises and will collaborate with the Hong Kong Trade Development Council in making the support deeper and broader.
It will integrate information related to the city’s professional sectors and publish lists of service providers supporting overseas expansion.
At the time, Cheung said the platform would also complement the Task Force on Supporting Mainland Enterprises in Going Global that launched in October.
The task force is one of the efforts outlined in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s policy address in September to help mainland enterprises build their operations overseas.

data.title: Chinese firms outpace US rivals in 2025 humanoid robot shipments, as AgiBot takes lead
data.description:
Shanghai-based AgiBot topped global humanoid robot shipments in 2025, with nearly a 38 per cent share, as China’s robotics firms dominated the market, leaving in the dust major US players like Elon Musk’s Tesla.
According to data released on Thursday by Omdia, AgiBot shipped 5,168 humanoid robots last year to lead five other Chinese companies in the research firm’s top 10 list.
Unitree Robotics, headquartered in Hangzhou, ranked second with 4,200 humanoids shipped last year, which accounted for 32 per cent of the market.
Hong Kong-listed UBTech Robotics, based in Shenzhen, was ranked third with 1,000 units shipped.
Leju Robotics, Engine AI and Fourier Intelligence ranked fourth, fifth and sixth in 2025 shipments, according to Omdia.
Shipments worldwide jumped nearly 480 per cent in 2025 to 13,318 units, according to Omdia. It projected the volume to reach 2.6 million units in 2035.
“Chinese vendors are setting benchmarks in large-scale production, as they have reached thousand-unit shipments in a short period, enabling the deployment of tens of thousands of robots annually,” Omdia analyst Lian Jye Su said in the report.

The 2025 shipment figures reflected how far Chinese manufacturers have pulled ahead in this fast-developing sector. These firms had issued five times as many related patents as the US over the past five years, Morgan Stanley said in its latest Robot Almanac.
By comparison, US robot maker Tesla shipped only 150 units in 2025, which accounted for 1 per cent of the global market, according to Omdia. American firms Figure AI and Agility Robotics also shipped 150 units each last year.
The scaled-up production by Chinese manufacturers was driven by a combination of favourable policies, infrastructure support and investments in the public and private sectors, Omdia’s Su said.
Embodied intelligence – a branch of AI that operates through a physical body – was identified by the Chinese government as a key future industry for the first time in a work report delivered at the annual meeting of the National People’s Congress last March.


With strong government support, China has witnessed a boom in the humanoid robot sector in recent years. The country’s economic planning agency, the National Development and Reform Commission, even flagged the risks of excessive duplication in the sector in November.
Major Chinese robotics firms have ramped up their commercialisation efforts amid growing competition. They have formed partnerships with the broader manufacturing industry to enable large-scale deployment of their robots on factory floors.
In August, AgiBot secured an order from car parts manufacturer Fulin Precision to deploy nearly 100 robots across its factories. In July, AgiBot and Unitree landed orders totalling 124 million yuan (US$17.7 million) from China Mobile.

data.title: Hong Kong just had its driest January day. But how does that impact your health?
data.description:
Hong Kong experienced its driest January day on record earlier this week, bucking its reputation for high temperatures and humidity.
Relative humidity in the city plunged to 17 per cent on Thursday, the lowest for the month since records started in 1984.
To help Hongkongers adjust, the Post speaks to experts about how to manage the cold and dry weather.
1. What is the impact on the respiratory system?
Dr Edmund Lam Wing-wo, a family doctor, said the sudden cold and dry weather was conducive to the proliferation of respiratory viruses and could weaken people’s immune systems, including their nasal mucosa response.
“If viruses become more rampant and people’s immune systems weaken, it becomes easier to catch infections ranging from colds to flu,” he said on Friday.
He noted that other infections, such as those caused by respiratory syncytial virus or rhinovirus, could also be more prevalent because of the dry and cold weather, despite being present year round.
Lam said wearing surgical masks outdoors could help, as the air people breathed in could be less dry and cold. He also reminded the public to put on enough clothing and drink enough water to stay hydrated.
Lam said those with asthma could keep their condition under control with the help of inhalers, but they should seek medical advice if their symptoms worsened.
He added that the weather could also worsen existing cases of respiratory illnesses, such as colds and coughs.

2. What about moisturising?
Lam said his family clinic had, by Friday, seen several patients whose skin was so itchy that they could not sleep or were bleeding after scratching themselves.
Such symptoms were particularly common among the elderly or those suffering from atopic eczema.
He reminded residents to moisturise their skin with creams and avoid using scorching hot water for showers despite the cold weather.
Those seeking medical advice could ask for ointments with steroids, which would not have any side effects if used for a short period, he said.
“But if you have not had medical advice and the itching is severe, you could visit a doctor. You may have wounded the skin through scratching and developed an infection, which may require treatment with antibiotics,” Lam said.
3. How can my diet help me stay hydrated?
Bonnie Leung Wing-yan, a dietitian from the Hong Kong Community Dietitian Association, stressed the importance of drinking water in a dry environment and suggested that daily consumption should be 30 to 35ml per kilogram of body weight.
She added that consuming omega-3 fatty acids could help maintain cell membrane integrity, reduce the loss of moisture, improve skin barrier function and hydration capacity. They can be found in salmon, yellow croaker, mackerel, flaxseed, chia seeds, walnuts and avocados.
Foods such as carrots and pumpkins, which are rich in vitamin A, could help maintain healthy skin epithelial cells and promote skin repair, while vitamin C-rich foods such as kiwi fruits and strawberries were good for collagen synthesis, she said.
The dietitian said nuts and vegetable oils, which contain a lot of vitamin E, could protect cell membranes and reduce skin moisture loss.

She added that different types of tea, including floral and fruit varieties, were also good for hydration.
Chinese-style soups rich in antioxidants and vitamins A and E were also good for dry and cold weather, including one cooked with lily bulbs, cashews, pumpkin and snow fungus, or those made with black sesame, black beans, walnuts and figs.
“When the weather is cold, the body might want to eat more to stay warm and could unintentionally consume too much,” she said.
“I suggest maintaining a balanced diet with primarily vegetables and proteins and less fat to avoid excess calories that could lead to extra pounds.”

data.title: Alibaba logistics unit Cainiao opens US-Mexico cross-border service
data.description:
Cainiao, the logistics arm of Alibaba Group Holding, is stepping up its overseas push with a new service crossing the US-Mexico border.
The cross-border logistics service, a first for Cainiao in the Americas, was designed to handle parcel flow between the two countries, which was one of the region’s high-traffic corridors, the Alibaba unit said on Friday. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
The service was expected to reach 99 per cent of the Mexico market and would be priced at around 60 per cent of the current market average, according to Cainiao.
The initiative aimed to “reduce the long-standing cost barrier for US-Mexico parcel delivery for platforms and sellers”, the company said.
Cainiao was expected to control key nodes including sorting, line-haul transfer and last-mile delivery for the service.
That would be based on the company’s ability to run the service with self-operated local networks – instead of outsourcing – in both the US and Mexico, according to Cainiao.

The launch highlights Cainiao’s ambition to position itself as an infrastructure provider within the US-Mexico trade corridor.
This development also formed part of Cainiao’s broader efforts to expand in major overseas markets by offering low-cost, tech-powered services. The firm said it expected 2026 to be “a breakout year for logistics technology”.
Mexico has remained the fastest-growing and most stable export destination for the US since 2020, according to the Centre for Strategic and International Studies, a Washington-based think tank. Its data showed that Mexico last August overtook Canada as the US’ largest export market.
Cainiao’s logistics technology solutions have been deployed in 27 countries and regions, which span 800 collaborative projects worldwide. Its global footprint covered the Americas, the Asia-Pacific, the Middle East and Europe, according to its official data.
Beyond the Americas launch, Cainiao had stepped up investments in Japan and South Korea. On Friday, Cainiao said it was boosting local delivery services in the two countries, with a new logistics tech office set up in Japan.
“As we continue to deepen our business in China, we will also scale in both emerging markets and key mature markets – including Japan, South Korea, the US and Europe – supported by an expanding global delivery and after-sales service network,” said Bi Jianghua, vice-president at Cainiao and general manager of Cainiao Logistics Technology.
In November, Cainiao expanded its parcel delivery service to eight African countries – including Morocco, Ghana and Nigeria – and planned to extend the service to South Africa and Egypt.

data.title: 1 dead, 38 missing in Philippines landfill collapse after garbage avalanche
data.description:
An avalanche of garbage and debris buried or trapped workers in low-slung buildings at a landfill in the Philippines, killing one person, injuring a dozen and leaving 38 others missing, officials said on Friday.
Dozens of rescuers retrieved 13 people alive overnight and were searching for the missing still trapped after the mountain of garbage, earth and debris collapsed on them on Thursday afternoon in the village of Binaliw in Cebu City, officials and police said. Landfill workers were among those affected, but it’s not clear if there were neighbouring residents or others involved as well.
One of those rescued, a female landfill worker, died while being brought to a hospital, regional police director Brig Gen Roderick Maranan told Associated Press, adding the rest survived with injuries and were hospitalised.
Jaylord Antigua, a 31-year-old office worker in the landfill, said the collapse of the mountain of garbage unfolded rapidly without warning and in good weather. The avalanche of rubbish destroyed his office, where he managed to extricate himself with bruises on his face and arms by crawling under the rubble and debris.
“I saw a light and crawled towards it in a hurry because I feared there will be more landslides,” Antigua said. “It was traumatic. I feared that it was my end so this is my second life.”

Search and rescue efforts were continuing for 38 missing, Cebu Mayor Nestor Archival and the Office of Civil Defense said on Friday.
“All response teams remain fully engaged in search and retrieval efforts to locate the remaining missing persons with strict adherence to safety protocols,” Archival said in a statement posted on Facebook.
“The city government assures the public and the families of those affected that all necessary measures are being taken to ensure safety, transparency, accountability and compassionate assistance as operations continue,” Archival said.
Pictures released by authorities showed rescuers with earth-moving equipment scouring a building devastated by the massive slide with its twisted tin roofs and iron beams.
Relatives waited in anguish as the search and rescue proceeded. A woman wept openly and asked the rescuers to speed up the search.
Authorities and officials at the waste management facility, which has 110 employees, were to hold an emergency meeting on Friday, Archival said.

One of the buildings hit by the wall of garbage that cascaded down in the landfill was a warehouse where workers separated recyclable waste and rubbish, Maranan said, adding that it remains unclear if nearby houses were also affected.
Such landfills and open dumpsites have long been a source of safety and health concerns in many cities and towns in the Philippines, especially in areas close to poor communities, where residents scavenge for junk and leftover food in the garbage heaps.
In July 2000, a huge mound of garbage in a dumpsite in a shantytown in suburban Quezon City in metropolitan Manila collapsed after days of stormy weather, and the avalanche also ignited a fire.
The disaster left more than 200 people dead and many more missing, damaged scores of shanties and prompted the enactment of a law, which required the closure of illegal dumpsites and better waste management by authorities.

data.title: China cites Biden’s message to Xi that Japan could build nuclear weapons ‘overnight’
data.description:
China released an official report on Thursday stating that Japan “may already have produced weapons-grade plutonium in secret” and “has the technological and economic capabilities to achieve nuclear armament in a short period of time”.
The report assessing Japan’s nuclear threat cited former US president Joe Biden’s direct message to Chinese President Xi Jinping that Japan had the capacity to have nuclear weapons “virtually overnight”.
Biden first disclosed the information in an interview with the American public broadcaster Public Broadcasting Service in June 2016.
“What happens if Japan, who could tomorrow, could go nuclear tomorrow? They have the capacity to do it virtually overnight,” Biden said when recalling a conversation he had with Xi, but he did not say when it took place.


Although some state media reported the interview, Chinese authorities have not responded to Biden’s remarks in public.
On Thursday, the 30-page report titled “Nuclear Ambitions of Japan’s Right-Wing Forces: A Serious Threat to World Peace” was released in Beijing and included a call for the international community to “take concrete and strong measures” against Japan’s escalating nuclear ambitions.
It comes as relations between China and Japan are at their lowest point in years. A senior Japanese official last month told local media that he believed Japan should own nuclear weapons, but Tokyo denied the claim represented Japan’s official stance.
The report was produced jointly by the China Arms Control and Disarmament Association (CACDA) and the Nuclear Strategic Planning Research Institute, a think tank affiliated with the China National Nuclear Corporation.
It suggested that Japan had secretly pursued the research and development of nuclear weapons during the second world war. “[Japan] has since established a comprehensive nuclear fuel cycle system and possesses robust nuclear industrial capabilities,” the report said.
The authors specifically highlighted Japan’s capabilities in manufacturing plutonium, a critical material for making bombs.
The report said Japan was the only non-nuclear state under the NPT (Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty) with spent nuclear fuel reprocessing technology and the capability to extract weapons-grade plutonium, which is recovered from uranium fuel irradiated for only two to three months in a plutonium production reactor.
“According to publicly available information from the Japan Atomic Energy Agency, Japan reprocessed spent fuel from the experimental fast reactor Joyo in 1984 to recover plutonium. The reactor’s initial core design included a breeding zone, and from its operation beginning in 1978 until the core design was changed in 1994, it technically had the potential to produce weapons-grade plutonium,” the report said.
“US experts have analysed that Japan likely used the Joyo experimental fast reactor to produce approximately 40kg [88lbs] of weapons-grade plutonium before the core design was modified,” it said, citing a 1994 Greenpeace study.
The report also said Japan had stockpiled plutonium far exceeding its needs for civilian nuclear power, creating what it said was a persistent and severe imbalance between the supply and demand of sensitive nuclear materials.
Furthermore, it assessed that Japan maintained operational platforms with nuclear weapons delivery capabilities and had the technological foundation to develop nuclear-powered submarines and aircraft carriers.
For decades, Japan has adhered to three non-nuclear principles – not possessing, producing or permitting the introduction of nuclear weapons – while relying on the United States for extended deterrence. Japan is also a signatory to the NPT, which commits non-nuclear states to forgo the development or acquisition of nuclear arms.
At the launch event for the report, Dai Huaicheng, CACDA’s secretary general, said that “for some time now, Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi has hinted at revising the three non-nuclear principles, with senior Japanese officials even going so far as to claim that Japan should possess nuclear weapons”.
Dai said such remarks revealed Japan’s ambitions “to break free from the constraints of the international order and accelerate the revival of militarism and remilitarisation”.
At a daily press briefing on Thursday, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said the report exposed the intention of Japanese right-wing forces to possess nuclear weapons. She said the report listed “the alarming facts” that Japan had secretly been conducting research on nuclear weapons and stockpiling large amounts of sensitive nuclear materials for a long time.
Some Chinese nuclear experts estimate that Japan has the political motivation, as well as the technical capacity, to develop nuclear weapons in less than three years.


An expert based in western China who asked not to be named due to the sensitivity of the topic said there was speculation that Japan already had two nuclear bombs.
“If they dare to pursue nuclear weapons, that number could grow to three or four. And nowadays, the yield of just one tactical nuclear weapon is 10 times that of the two bombs they once received,” he said, referring to the bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in World War II.
On Tuesday, China’s Ministry of Commerce announced a ban on the export of “dual-use” products to end users linked to the Japanese military. The export of items with both commercial and military applications which could “contribute to enhancing Japan’s military capabilities” is prohibited.
A Beijing-based nuclear expert, who was not authorised to speak to the media and also requested anonymity, said on Friday: “The government has clearly recognised the urgency of the situation. If we don’t act now, Japan will go nuclear”.

data.title: Why From Dusk Till Dawn has aged poorly as the vampire movie with George Clooney turns 30
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in our From the Vault feature series, in which we reflect on culturally significant movies celebrating notable anniversaries.
In recent months, Quentin Tarantino has been in the news for criticising actors such as Paul Dano and Matthew Lillard in a manner unbecoming of a cinephile. But there was a time he fancied himself a leading man – or at least, the leading man’s creepy brother.
Directed by Robert Rodriguez (Desperado), from a script by Tarantino – based on a treatment by special effects expert Robert Kurtzman – From Dusk Till Dawn turns 30 this month. Although it contains all the hallmarks of a typical 1990s Tarantino crime caper, it switches gears halfway, morphing into a gory vampire flick.
George Clooney, still on his way to mega-stardom, plays bank robber Seth Gecko, on the run after a jailbreak staged by his brother, Richie (Tarantino). Even with a tattoo snaking up his neck, Clooney does not quite convince as a cold-blooded killer, but his charisma goes some way to masking the movie’s flaws.


Surprisingly, for those who have suffered through his cameos, Tarantino is well cast as the psychotic sex offender Richie. But compared to Clooney’s effortless star wattage, he is still, to quote his assessment of Dano, “weak sauce”.
If Tarantino casting himself as a sex offender seems strange, Rodriguez compounds the problem by having the camera mimic Richie’s malevolent gaze. The fact that their boss was Miramax’s Harvey Weinstein makes the decision even more uncomfortable.
But then, From Dusk Till Dawn always plays to the lowest common denominator, moving from juvenile to downright sleazy. One wonders what the ever-classy Clooney made of it all.
The film starts in familiar Tarantino territory with the Geckos and their hostage Gloria (Brenda Hillhouse) hiding from the cops at a Texas roadside store.

Anchored by a long back-and-forth between a Texas Ranger (Michael Parks) and the store owner (John Hawkes) that turns into a larger-than-life shoot-out, this opening sequence is tense and funny, with witty reversals, profane dialogue and plenty of pop culture references.
While nothing hints at the supernatural carnage to come, watching the owner firing a gun while engulfed in flames suggests there will be no holding back.
Sure enough, the next sequence has Richie, left alone with the hostage at a motel, sexually assaulting and killing her off-screen. “That’s not how it’s done!” Seth shouts, as if Richie has broken the criminals’ code rather than committed an unforgivable act of violence.
Perhaps it is meant to be daring, but it just feels crass, like the filmmakers do not really care about their female characters or understand the gravity of what they are showing.

The troubling tone continues as the brothers kidnap ex-preacher Jacob (Harvey Keitel) and his children, Scott (Ernest Liu) and Kate (Juliette Lewis), forcing them to drive the pair to Mexico, with Richie clearly leering at Kate.
One ill-judged moment shows her propositioning him – in his mind. Another fetishises shots of her feet. But are these Richie’s preoccupations or Tarantino’s?
Once across the border, the group stops at the Titty Twister bar, where all hell breaks loose. After a risqué dance by Santanico Pandemonium (Salma Hayek), the staff turn into vampires, attacking and killing the patrons. Led by Seth and Jacob, a group of survivors attempts to last the night.
This is where the film really goes off the rails – and not in the way its makers intended. The writing is lazy and self-referential, such as introducing a character called Sex Machine (special effects legend Tom Savini), whose codpiece revolver is a nod to Rodriguez’s earlier work.

With the bar full of B-movie actors (stoner comedian Cheech Marin, blaxploitation star Fred Williamson) and semi-naked women, it resembles a Tarantino fever dream.
Despite stitching two different genres together, it seems Tarantino and Rodriguez did not give much thought to either, leaving the whole enterprise feeling rushed and slapdash.
Although the practical effects are good, the digital ones have aged terribly. The creatures have no lore, no mystique; they just appear and attack. And, as nothing has been invested in bringing them to life, it is hard to be engaged in watching them die.
Upon release, the film made a respectable US$59 million on a budget of US$19 million, and has since become a cult favourite – especially among men of a certain age. Two sequels, a television series and a video game followed, but the film’s greatest legacy is Ryan Coogler’s 2025 hit Sinners.

A film about criminals and vampires – but for adults – Sinners has the rare distinction of actually having something to say. Before it came out, Coogler wrote an open letter to thank everyone who had inspired him, Tarantino included. Perhaps he could teach Tarantino a thing or two about grace.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Ex-commerce chief Frederick Ma urges support for Hong Kong special needs education
data.description:
Former commerce chief Frederick Ma Si-hang has urged the public to deepen their support for disabled students in the city at a charity golf event organised by the Hong Kong Special Schools Council to raise awareness for this group.
The tournament, held on Friday at the Fanling Golf Club, drew 21 teams of 84 people, including one consisting of players with disabilities. Participants competed to win prizes such as a BYD electric car and tickets to see Cantopop singer Jacky Cheung Hok-yau in concert.
“The event is more than just money. We hope to raise public awareness for special schools … children with special needs are in need of much more care and support,” said Ma, who also chairs the special schools council and the Hong Kong Trade Development Council.
“The council did not have a very concrete goal this year. The ultimate goal for us is to call on more and more people to pay attention to children with special needs. The more, the merrier.”
The Hong Kong Special Schools Council is a non-profit organisation dedicated to advancing special education and supporting students with special needs.
Ma did not disclose how much funds had been raised from the tournament, whose participants included leaders from several financial institutions such as HSBC, the city’s biggest lender, and FWD Group, where Ma serves as chairman.
The Post learned that part of the participation fee would serve as a fund to support 62 schools that provide education to children with special needs.
According to Ma, the money will support events organised by the schools.
The council raised around HK$800,000 (US$102,680) last year through members’ donations. It was used to support 30 physically challenged children and their carers to go on a trip to Beijing.
“It was not easy to organise tours for children with special needs, as they could not go alone. They need their carers, which means the group would become bigger and need more money,” Ma said.
Andreas Ma Ho-him, 13, was one of the special needs players who joined the tournament. Starting playing golf at the age of three, the teenager said he had a blast on Friday.
“I really like the tournament. Thanks to the council, physically challenged people could get to experience something that they might not have experienced before and expand their visions,” he said.
